name: Ironical Remark
strauss_status: EXPLICITLY NAMED — REQUIRES FURTHER DOCUMENTARY DEVELOPMENT

source.primary_work: Persecution and the Art of Writing
source.primary_essay: The Literary Character of the Guide for the Perplexed
source.source_formulation: Strauss names "ironical remarks" among Maimonides' devices without elaborating their varieties here.
source.source_mechanism: The apparent meaning of a statement differs from the meaning intended for the attentive reader.
source.documentary_limit: The present passage names the device but does not provide a complete operational account or a taxonomy of kinds of irony.

mechanism: A recoverable remark has a viable surface proposition, yet contextual, dramatic, argumentative, lexical, or architectonic evidence supports a materially different nonliteral proposition. The divergence may involve reversal, qualification, exposure, or distancing; it must not be reduced automatically to saying the exact opposite.
investigative_requirement: Preserve the literal wording; reconstruct the surface proposition before proposing a nonliteral reading; identify the speaker, target, object, and context; record each textual marker supporting divergence; test whether quotation, humor, sarcasm, parody, hyperbole, understatement, ambiguity, dramatic characterization, translation, or ordinary inconsistency better explains the passage; and never convert contextual tension into proof of authorial intention.
documentary_constraints: The present source names ironical remarks but does not classify their subtypes. Local descriptive labels remain noncanonical.
A candidate ironic reading must preserve the exact surface wording and must be stated separately from the literal proposition.
The nonliteral proposition need not be the exact logical negation of the surface proposition, but it must be materially distinct.
A contextual contradiction, absurdity, excessive praise, understatement, or tonal mismatch is a possible marker, not proof of irony.
Irony attributed to a character, narrator, quoted authority, or interlocutor must not be attributed automatically to the work's author.
The evaluator may corroborate a surface/nonliteral divergence structure but may not infer intended meaning, hidden teaching, ridicule, or truth.

trigger_conditions: A bounded remark is recoverable in a stable witness.
A literal surface proposition can be reconstructed.
A materially distinct candidate nonliteral proposition can be reconstructed without changing the words.
At least one documentary marker supports divergence between surface and candidate nonliteral propositions.
The proposed divergence is not merely disagreement with the surface proposition.
minimum_evidence: Exact remark text and stable witness location.
Declared textual boundary of the remark.
Documented relation between surface and nonliteral propositions.
Speaker, narrator, quotation, objection, hypothetical, and dramatic-role attribution.
Proposed target or object of the remark, preserved as unresolved when uncertain.
Every textual, contextual, tonal, dramatic, argumentative, or architectonic marker.
Source-language, translation, punctuation, edition, and textual-variant review.
Testing of literal coherence before any nonliteral reading is advanced.
An auditable evidence path from witness text through both proposition records to the divergence claim.
corroboration_indicators: The speaker elsewhere explicitly rejects the surface proposition.
The remark conflicts with a stable position documented elsewhere while the nonliteral reading resolves the conflict.
A repeated ironic pattern occurs under comparable dramatic or argumentative conditions.
The wording contains controlled exaggeration, praise, understatement, or incongruity independently supported by context.
The target responds as though the remark has a nonliteral force.
Architectonic placement or another independently reconstructed literary device converges on the same divergence.
A source comparison shows deliberate retention or alteration that intensifies the nonliteral effect.
ordinary_alternatives: Sarcasm or ridicule without broader doctrinal significance.
Humor, wit, parody, or comic exaggeration.
Quotation, objection, hypothetical, or reported opinion.
Change of audience, scope, time, condition, or definition.
Ordinary inconsistency, imprecision, or compositional revision.
The investigator has projected tone not supported by the text.
disqualifying_conditions: No stable remark or textual boundary can be recovered.
The surface proposition has not been reconstructed.
The candidate nonliteral proposition requires changing or adding words without evidence.
The two propositions are materially equivalent.
No documentary marker supports nonliteral divergence.
Literal coherence remains complete and no counterevidence exists.
Quotation, speaker attribution, genre, humor, hyperbole, ambiguity, translation, or witness variation fully explains the effect.
The analysis depends solely on perceived tone.
The nonliteral reading is preferred only because it is more unconventional or philosophically attractive.
The passage is attributed to the author without resolving dramatic or quoted voice.
authorized_response: Preserve the exact remark and its full context.
Create separate surface and candidate nonliteral proposition records.
Record the divergence relation without collapsing it into exact negation.
Document speaker, target, object, audience, and dramatic role separately.
Test literal coherence and all ordinary rhetorical, dramatic, lexical, textual, and compositional alternatives.
Search the work for parallel remarks, explicit corrections, reactions, and counterexamples.
Evaluate LC-006, LC-007, LC-010, LC-012, LC-020, or LC-021 separately when independently triggered.
Preserve intention and intended meaning as unresolved.
prohibited_inferences: Do not infer irony from disagreement with the literal statement.
Do not infer irony from tone alone.
Do not assume irony always means the exact opposite.
Do not treat humor, sarcasm, hyperbole, or ambiguity automatically as irony.
Do not attribute a character's irony directly to the author.
Do not infer the intended meaning or intended audience.
Do not infer concealment, hidden teaching, ridicule, or doctrinal truth.
Do not create canonical irony subtypes from the present source.
Do not collapse LC-011 into adjacent techniques.
operational.uncertainty_rule: When a material surface/nonliteral divergence is plausible but the speaker, target, markers, literal coherence, or ordinary alternatives remain contested, preserve a candidate ironic divergence with both proposition records and all rival explanations. Do not identify the intended meaning.
operational.termination_rule: Terminate as NOT_TRIGGERED when no materially distinct, textually supported nonliteral proposition is present; as BLOCKED_MISSING_EVIDENCE when source, boundary, context, voice, propositions, markers, or provenance are incomplete; as candidate when ordinary alternatives or corroboration remain unresolved; and as corroborated only when the surface/nonliteral divergence survives full documentary and adversarial testing. No outcome establishes intention or truth.

version_history.change_note: Initial executable projection of LC-011 with separate surface and nonliteral proposition records, marker and countermarker evidence, speaker and target controls, literal-coherence testing, documentary subtype restraint, and nonintentional epistemic limits.
